items.title: Pop, Bang, Fizzle! The rupee’s comeback has proved fleeting. Despite the Reserve Bank of India’s “bazooka” package to support the currency, it has...
items.description_html:
Pop, Bang, Fizzle!

The rupee’s comeback has proved fleeting.

Despite the Reserve Bank of India’s “bazooka” package to support the currency, it has slipped back above 96 to the dollar, edging closer to its record low of 96.9 in late May.

It isn’t that the central bank’s measures have failed. But markets may have got ahead of themselves. And geopolitical tensions, higher crude prices and a stronger dollar have returned, reminding investors that India’s external position remains vulnerable to global shocks.

Three things to know about the rupee’s recovery path.

👇
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-07-17/why-india-s-rupee-recovery-has-petered-out?srnd=undefined

items.title: Behind this is a composite collection of analytical lenses applied across years of annual reports, earnings transcripts and other public information t...
items.description_html:
Behind this is a composite collection of analytical lenses applied across years of annual reports, earnings transcripts and other public information to produce a consistent scorecard. The score itself isn't the point. The relative ranking often is.

So far, the highest score awarded is 83/100. That's intentional. The framework is deliberately stringent. It's still being refined through repeated stress tests and real-world application. It's a living system of many lenses.

Absolutely no recommendation!

items.title: BREAKING: US margin debt jumped +$86.5 billion in June, to a record $1.5 trillion. This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly increase, totaling +$281.2 b...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: US margin debt jumped +$86.5 billion in June, to a record $1.5 trillion.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly increase, totaling +$281.2 billion, or +23%.

Margin debt has surged +$494.1 billion, or +49%, over the last 12 months.

Meanwhile, a broader measure of investor leverage, which subtracts cash held in brokerage accounts from total margin borrowing, is up to ~1.4% of the S&P 500 market cap, near the highest on record.

This is in-line with the 2018 peak levels and exceeds the 2000 Dot-Com bubble peak of ~1.1%.

US investors have never been more leveraged.

items.title: Japanese retail investors are increasingly betting against the US Dollar: Net dollar short positions held by Japanese retail traders are up to $17.2 b...
items.description_html:
Japanese retail investors are increasingly betting against the US Dollar:

Net dollar short positions held by Japanese retail traders are up to $17.2 billion, the highest since records began in 2008.

This figure has more than quadrupled from month-over-month.

The surge reflects growing expectations that Japanese authorities will continue supporting the Yen through intervention, encouraging retail traders to position for a weaker US Dollar.

This comes after the Ministry of Japan spent ~$73.6 billion to support the Yen between April 28th and May 27th, with the Yen still falling more than 4% from its May high.

Retail investors in Japan believe the US Dollar rally is nearing its end.

• Industry landscape and outlook

• Impact of evolving regulatory changes

Be part of the conversation and gain valuable insights into the future of the stockbroking industry.

items.description_html:
RT Surya
what i liked most is this statement , need to work lot on this :

Most investors get this backwards. They hold their losers with iron discipline and sell their winners at the first sign of doubt.


Antifragile Thinking: The Courage to Change Your Mind

Conviction isn't a promise to stay bullish. It's a promise to follow the evidence. I've been bullish on CDMO through Neuland, Sai Life, Anthem Bio, Piramal, Laurus, Divis and others. I'll stay bullish only as long as the evidence justifies it.

items.title: RT Capitalmind: Re A few good active funds pull up the average performance of the category while the rest lag. So the problem isn't that active manage...
items.description_html:
RT Capitalmind
Re A few good active funds pull up the average performance of the category while the rest lag.

So the problem isn't that active management doesn't work. It clearly does in some segments of the market as we saw above. The problem is that you may not have a system that alerts you to take action when your fund starts underperforming.

That's what All Weather Equity at our Capitalmind PMS is built to do. A rules-based strategy investing in passive and active mutual funds across market caps having delivered 16.7% CAGR vs the Nifty 50 TRI's 12% (as of 15 Jul'26).

Read the full article by @investkahakare with more insights here: https://www.capitalmind.in/insights/are-you-holding-an-underperforming-mutual-fund

items.title: RT Shagun: India’s kharif season had a tough start this year. Here is the list of 315 districts the govt flagged as potentially affected by El Niño ...
items.description_html:
RT Shagun
India’s kharif season had a tough start this year. Here is the list of 315 districts the govt flagged as potentially affected by El Niño 2026 -- and the 111 high-priority ones among them, where irrigation coverage is under 25%.

The story has a searchable district table and an interactive map to look up where things stand.

We analysed IMD rainfall data for all 111 and saw that 69 of them are already recording deficient rainfall. Farmers here have almost nothing to fall back on if the monsoon underperforms, and kharif sowing is already 16% behind last year.
https://www.google.com/amp/s/www.downtoearth.org.in/amp/story/agriculture/these-are-the-111-districts-the-government-flagged-as-vulnerable-to-el-ni%25C3%25B1o-2026-and-69-are-already-rainfall-deficient


Down To Earth: These are the 111 districts the government flagged as vulnerable to El Niño 2026 — and 69 are already rainfall deficient.

Nearly six in 10 of India's most vulnerable farm districts are already receiving below-normal rainfall, disrupting kharif sowing and increasing the risk of

items.title: RT CSEINDIA: As India pushes ahead with its first interlinking of rivers project, tribal communities in Madhya Pradesh say they are paying the price. ...
items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
As India pushes ahead with its first interlinking of rivers project, tribal communities in Madhya Pradesh say they are paying the price. Villages are being demolished, families displaced, and questions over rehabilitation, compensation, and environmental safeguards continue to grow.

The renewed Pyre Protest reflects the anger and uncertainty of those who claim they are being uprooted without fair justice. While authorities say the process is consensual, many affected residents tell a different story.

Can development be considered successful if the people most affected feel unheard? Share your thoughts below.

Watch the full video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9W3ukrYqdc4

@down2earthindia | @sunitanar | @bsrivas | @JoelMichaelhps | @surangya

items.title: Sunil Singhania Sir - "The first time we looked at Bajaj Finance, it was like a 400 Cr mcap, but it had the potential of generating or reaching up to ...
items.description_html:
Sunil Singhania Sir - "The first time we looked at Bajaj Finance, it was like a 400 Cr mcap, but it had the potential of generating or reaching up to Rs 500 crores of profits in the next 5 years.

So, that was the thought process, and we bought into it; we built a small position. When they did the first QIP, we raised our exposure to almost 8-9 percent in the company. But this is one of those companies, where we bought in early, but we booked profit also too early.

Bajaj Finance is a 6.54 Lakh cr company today! The stake value would have been 59000 Cr today. Abbakus' total AUM is 36000 cr as of March 2026

The intent is not to point this out as a mistake (learnt a lot from Sunil Sir's interviews), but the fact that everyone does miss out on something, as long as you learn from it, you become a better investor!

no stock reco

items.title: CFR's Shannon O'Neil argues that "With the US backing away from its once-staunch defense of the rules-based trading order, Europe bears a heavier burd...
items.description_html:
CFR's Shannon O'Neil argues that "With the US backing away from its once-staunch defense of the rules-based trading order, Europe bears a heavier burden in taking on and excluding those that game it."

She is probably right. The only way to save the global rules-based trading regime is to restructure it along the lines proposed by JM Keynes at Bretton Woods, in which trade partners are forced to restrict their ability to externalize the costs of their domestic policy distortions by running large, persistent trade surpluses. Without such restrictions, we are back in the world Joan Robinson warned about, where the rising costs of beggar-thy-neighbor trade surpluses eventually force a breakdown in global trade.
@shannonkoneil https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-07-17/europe-can-t-save-rules-based-trade-without-taking-on-china?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

24 hours to lift-off. 🚀

Eight years ago, @SkyrootA started with just two people with an idea.

There was no policy framework for private spaceflight in India, no real funding ecosystem for space startups, and we were taking on one of the world’s hardest engineering challenges. The odds of success seemed incredibly small.

Today, India’s first privately developed orbital rocket stands on the launch pad, ready for lift-off.

This moment belongs to every member of our team, our investors, the Government of India,@PMOIndia, @isro, @INSPACeIND, our partners, suppliers, customers, and everyone who believed in us long before this day.

We have done everything we possibly could.

Now we let physics do its job.

Whatever tomorrow brings, every second of flight will teach us something invaluable. Reaching the launch pad and attempting an orbital launch is a landmark milestone for India—and an achievement only a handful of companies worldwide have reached. Every milestone we cross will advance not just Skyroot or India’s space journey, but the global commercial space ecosystem.

Tomorrow, we take the next step. 🇮🇳🚀

items.title: Caixin: "Chinese households are spending the smallest share of their incomes since the depths of the pandemic. While per capita disposable income rose...
items.description_html:
Caixin: "Chinese households are spending the smallest share of their incomes since the depths of the pandemic. While per capita disposable income rose 4.2% year-on-year in real terms in the first half of the year, consumer spending lagged far behind with just a 2.7% increase."

Caixin notes that while households consumed 68-69% of their income before the pandemic, they are now consuming less than 65%, even as banks are under pressure to expand consumer lending. This probably reflects rising worries about job prospects.

For all the policy talk about boosting the role of consumption in driving growth, in other words, the economy is moving in the opposite direction. Not only is disposable household income rising more slowly in real terms than than GDP (4.7% in the first six months of 2026), but households are also saving a higher share of their income.

As the household share of GDP declines, there are only two ways to balance this. One way is for the investment share to rise, which hasn't been happening recently and, given how few productive investment opportunities remain in China, is unlikely to happen without an even greater surge in debt.

The other way, of course, is for the share of net exports in driving GDP to rise.
https://www.caixinglobal.com/2026-07-16/chinese-consumers-tighten-purse-strings-despite-modest-income-gains-102464740.html?cxg=web&Sfrom=twitter

items.title: RT menaka doshi: Hold the Trade Deal Bubbly On Wednesday, I woke up to front-page newspaper advertisements announcing that the India-UK trade deal was...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
Hold the Trade Deal Bubbly

On Wednesday, I woke up to front-page newspaper advertisements announcing that the India-UK trade deal was going live that day.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi hailed the day on social media, while export lobbies such as the gems and jewelry industry invited the press to witness the first shipment under the agreement.

I can’t recall the last time a trade deal activation received such fanfare.

Sure, the India-UK Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement is, as I wrote here last year, genuinely comprehensive.

Even so, the deal’s greatest impact may lie less in what it immediately does for exports than in what it does for imports.

The UK government estimates its exports to India to rise by nearly 60% in the long run – this is equivalent to an additional £15.7 billion of UK exports to India when applied to projections of future trade in 2040.

UK imports from India are likely to rise by 25% in the long run – this is equivalent to £9.8 billion in additional UK imports from India when applied to projections of future trade in 2040.

That is the real policy watershed. India is choosing greater openness and competition. That resolve will be tested frequently, like now.

India Edition 👇

https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-07-15/the-real-value-of-the-india-uk-trade-deal?srnd=undefined

items.title: We initiated Sansera for our subscribers around 16th December 2025. But the more important thing is how we filtered on this idea. > First key factor w...
items.description_html:
We initiated Sansera for our subscribers around 16th December 2025.

But the more important thing is how we filtered on this idea.

> First key factor we observed was the product mix change, where the company's intent was to gradually move from its ICE component business to Aero/Defence/Semicon (ADS) business.

> This product mix shift was visible from the fact that margins had been improving over the last 2 quarters, and growth was starting to pick up. That's how it came under our filters.

> Going a little deeper, we understood that the kind of things it was doing in the ADS business were quite moated. It had a single-source monopoly on Airbus A350/A320 thrust-reversal gimbals with zero defects over 12 years; hogging (machining from solid billet vs. forging) is a much harder-to-replicate capability for aerospace tolerances.

> Also, in its semiconductor business, it was working with Lam Research, where this year their business is expected to go from $10M to $30M .

> Also, as per our channel checks, they had orders/interest from Rolls-Royce for aero-engine feelers, the MMRFIC JV (navy fuses, radars), and a Tesla ESS order.

> All these things were a confirmation of our thesis that management has the capabilities, and that the product mix is changing for good.

> They have also given strong guidance of ₹8,000 Cr in revenue, with ADS at 20–25% of overall business and blended EBITDA margins moving above the 20% mark.

On top of this, we also visited their facility, where we got to understand the manufacturing complexity involved in the components they make for their ADS business, and their capabilities in precision engineering.

The framework was simple: through scanning, found a business where earnings were breaking out and margins were improving > went through their earnings calls and found the story of the product mix change > mapped out the value chain and tried to understand Sansera's moat in the new business they were focusing on > visited their facility to verify our thesis on the company.

Disclaimer: This is not a recommendation to buy or sell. We continue to hold it in our recommendation portfolio and may change our view at any point in time.

items.title: RT Data For India: New Data Byte: The choice of baseline affects how we see warming, by Juhi Chatterjee One of the best measures to understand warming...
items.description_html:
RT Data For India
New Data Byte: The choice of baseline affects how we see warming, by Juhi Chatterjee

One of the best measures to understand warming is through temperature anomalies. A temperature anomaly is the departure of the temperature for a particular year from a baseline. Positive anomalies indicate warmer than average conditions, while negative anomalies indicate cooler conditions when compared to the baseline.

This baseline or reference period is also referred to as the Long Period Average (LPA), and represents the average climatic conditions over a multi-decadal period of typically 30 years when measuring changes in temperature.

Temperatures today are typically measured against a reference period of 1991-2020, which is the baseline now in use by the India Meteorological Department, the official source of climatological data in India. However, looking at a range of baselines can provide important insights.

Using older baselines, such as 1951-1980, produces larger positive anomalies for present day temperatures with the anomaly estimated to be 0.98°C in 2024. More recent baselines, such as 1991-2020 produce smaller anomaly values for the same year with anomalies reducing to around 0.65°C. An intermediate baseline of 1981-2010 shows warming of a magnitude in between the two at approximately 0.78°C for 2024.

Updating baselines is important for climate monitoring because newer reference periods represent the current climatic conditions experienced by populations better. At the same time, older baselines highlight the cumulative scale of long-term warming. All baselines, however, show a warming trend.

#Climate #Temperature #Heat #India #DataForIndia

items.title: China's economic slowdown is deepening: China's GDP grew just +4.3% YoY in Q2 2026, the slowest pace since Q4 2022 and below the government's official...
items.description_html:
China's economic slowdown is deepening:

China's GDP grew just +4.3% YoY in Q2 2026, the slowest pace since Q4 2022 and below the government's official target range of +4.5% to +5.0%.

This followed +5.0% expansion in Q1, bringing first-half growth of the year to +4.7% YoY.

Furthermore, fixed-asset investment fell -5.7% YoY in the H1, worsening from a -4.1% decline in the first 5 months.

The decline was driven by property investment plunging -18%, the largest drop on record dating back to 1992.

At the same time, retail sales increased +1% YoY in June after a -0.6% decline in May, while auto purchases dropped more than -16%.

Weakness is spreading across the world’s 2nd-largest economy.

items.title: The richest Americans have never controlled this much wealth: The wealth of the top 0.00001% of Americans is up to a record ~12% of US national income...
items.description_html:
The richest Americans have never controlled this much wealth:

The wealth of the top 0.00001% of Americans is up to a record ~12% of US national income.

National income measures the total income earned across the economy, including wages, business profits, and investment income.

This percentage has more than quadrupled since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

By comparison, this metric never exceeded 1% between the 1950s and the 1990s.

The surge has been fueled by record gains in the equity market and rising real estate prices.

Asset owners are the only winners.

items.description_html:
BREAKING: Foreign holdings of US Treasuries rose +$18 billion in May, to $9.37 trillion, the 2nd-highest on record.

Over the last 12 months, total foreign holdings of US Treasuries have surged +$549 billion.

Japan, the largest foreign holder, reduced its holdings -$67 billion, to $1.14 trillion, the lowest since May 2025.

The UK, the 2nd-largest holder, added +$11 billion, to a record $949 billion.

China, the 3rd-largest holder, raised its holdings +$8 billion, to $659 billion, the highest since February.

Meanwhile, the 7 largest financial centers added +$33 billion, bringing their total Treasury holdings to $3.24 trillion, an all-time high.

Demand for US Treasuries remains strong.

items.description_html:
Unprofitable small-cap stocks are skyrocketing:

Russell 2000 companies with negative earnings per share (EPS) have returned +154% since mid-2025.

Over the same timeperiod, firms with positive EPS have gained just +34%.

Year-to-date, unprofitable companies in the index have surged +45%, beating the +18% gain of profitable firms.

As a result, the Russell 2000 has rallied +20% so far in 2026, on track for its best year since 2003.

By comparison, the S&P 500 has risen +11% while the Magnificent 7 has gained just +4%.

This comes as the best-performing small-cap stocks have been technology and infrastructure companies positioned to benefit from AI spending.

The market is rewarding AI exposure, regardless of profitability.

items.title: RT Carolyn Rockey: Jo Nagai was raising swallowtail butterflies at his home in Kobe, Japan, when he noticed something odd. The ones he had looked afte...
items.description_html:
RT Carolyn Rockey
Jo Nagai was raising swallowtail butterflies at his home in Kobe, Japan, when he noticed something odd. The ones he had looked after as caterpillars seemed to recognize him. Wild butterflies fled. His didn't.
He was in second grade. He wrote a four-page letter to Dr. Martha Weiss, an entomologist at Georgetown University who had studied whether moths could retain memories through metamorphosis. He asked if she could help him design a version of her experiment for butterflies.
She said yes.
Using a muscle therapy device, Jo trained caterpillars to associate the scent of lavender with a mild vibration. When the caterpillars became butterflies, 70 per cent of them still avoided the lavender. Their brains had been completely rebuilt during metamorphosis. The memory survived anyway.
Then he bred them.
The offspring, which had never been trained, also avoided lavender. So did their grandchildren. Without ever experiencing the vibration, two generations of butterflies inherited an aversion to a scent their grandmother had been taught to fear.
Jo documented it all in a 33-page research paper and presented his findings at the International Congress of Entomology in Kobe in 2024. He was 10.
A second grader wrote a letter to a Georgetown professor, and together they found evidence that butterflies can pass memories down through generations.

-Wilderness Whisper

items.title: RT lordmicky 🇵🇹: Arthur Conan Doyle, creator of Sherlock Holmes, once traveled to Switzerland. When he arrived, he took a taxi and the driver to...
items.description_html:
RT lordmicky 🇵🇹
Arthur Conan Doyle, creator of Sherlock Holmes, once traveled to Switzerland. When he arrived, he took a taxi and the driver told him he wouldn't charge him if he autographed a book.

Conan Doyle, surprised, asked the taxi driver how he knew he was a writer, and the taxi driver replied: "Easy, your shoes are covered in dust that isn't from here. From the pattern of your shoes, I see that they are English shoes. So it's English dust. You have an ink stain on your toes, so you are a writer, a British writer."

Shocked, Conan Doyle replied: "You are smarter than Sherlock Holmes."

"Yes sir, and your suitcases clearly have Arthur Conan Doyle written on them."

items.title: RT Paras Chopra: Valuations are fundamentally grounded in expectations of future profit across a company’s lifetime, and these profits depend on moat...
items.description_html:
RT Paras Chopra
Valuations are fundamentally grounded in expectations of future profit across a company’s lifetime, and these profits depend on moats.

The crazy $1T valuation of foundation model companies seem overblown precisely because a lot of their revenue comes from API usage, which is one line in code away from switching to a significantly cheaper but almost as good open models.

Historically, the high valuations of FAANG are all due to moats such as network effects or switching costs.

Even nvidia’s valuation makes sense. CUDA has incredible ecosystem and once you buy a chip, you can switch out easily.

But, more and more, it feels frontier models are like a commodity with only minute differences between them. What justifies a trillion dollar valuation for them?

items.title: Polycab India Limited | Q1 FY27 Earnings Domestic Demand Supports Sustained Expansion ▪The Company reported robust consolidated revenue growth of 39%...
items.description_html:
Polycab India Limited | Q1 FY27 Earnings

Domestic Demand Supports Sustained Expansion

▪The Company reported robust consolidated revenue growth of 39% YoY in Q1FY27, reflecting continued strong momentum across its W&C and FMEG segments.

▪EBITDA grew by 32% YoY during the quarter, supported by improved operational efficiency, and a favourable business mix

▪PAT registered a growth of 33%, while PAT margin stood at 9.7%

▪The domestic W&C business continued its strong performance, recording 39% YoY growth during the quarter. Growth was driven by resilient demand, improved execution under Project Spring, and higher commodity prices.

▪Wires outpaced cables during the quarter, supported by strong on-ground execution.

▪The international business recorded a 13% YoY degrowth; however, diversified global footprint and a healthy order book underscore strong forward momentum.

▪The margins improved sequentially, aided by a favourable business mix and operational efficiencies, even as international business contribution moderated.

Results sourced from @stockscansin

items.title: Housing has been sold again. This time to BSE-listed Aurum PropTech for AUD$68 million (Rs 458 crore) in an all-stock deal. Housing was acquired by Pr...
items.description_html:
Housing has been sold again.

This time to BSE-listed Aurum PropTech for AUD$68 million (Rs 458 crore) in an all-stock deal.

Housing was acquired by PropTiger in 2017 through a stock deal. PropTiger was later acquired by REA Group Australia, which is owned by Rupert Murdoch's News Corp.

Following the transaction, REA will own a 25% stake in Aurum PropTech.

The India business of REA generated around AUD$62 million (Rs 418 crore) in revenue in FY26.

But it was expected to lose about AUD$36 million (Rs 243 crore) of EBITDA annually.

REA expects to book an accounting loss of around AUD$110 million (Rs 741 crore) on the sale.

items.title: Steady Set from Groww, Angel One & Anand Rathi Share - all three are showing one common trend: Broking is no longer just broking MTF, wealth managemen...
items.description_html:
Steady Set from Groww, Angel One & Anand Rathi Share - all three are showing one common trend:

Broking is no longer just broking

MTF, wealth management, AMC, credit, mutual fund distribution & other financial products are increasingly becoming the monetisation and retention layers

Anand Rathi Share & Stock Brokers

Cons. REV grew 22% YoY to 246 Cr, while EBITDA grew 30% to 97 Cr.

PAT before the exceptional item increased 71% YoY to 39 Cr.

However, reported PAT increased only 2% YoY to 23 Cr because of an exceptional fraud compensation expense of 21 Cr.

The exceptional item relates to fraudulent off market transfers from the demat accounts of two clients in the company’s Depository Participant business.

It has compensated the affected clients & has filed an FIR with the Economic Offences Wing.

EOW has traced the money trail and attached certain assets and bank accounts of the beneficiaries.

Company has also filed insurance claims, and any recovery will be recognised once realisation becomes reasonably certain.

EBITDA margin improved to 39.5% from 37.2% last year.

The company’s AUC increased 21% YoY to 1.13 lakh Cr, while distribution AUM increased 26% to 9,479 Cr.

MTF book remained the strongest growth engine.

MTF outstanding increased 55% YoY to 1,332 Cr, while MTF interest income grew 52% to 43 Cr.

This indicates that both broking & non-broking businesses contributed to growth.

Broking & related services contributed around 52% of REV vs 55% YoY. The growing contribution from non-broking segments is gradually derisking the business model.

Distribution income increased 31% YoY to 28 Cr.

Mutual funds contributed 76% of distribution AUM, while PMS & AIF products account for the remaining 24%.

More than 70% of active clients come from Tier 2 & Tier 3 cities & 57% of clients have been associated with the company for more than 3 years, while 43% have relationships exceeding 5 years.

Company has also approved the incorporation of a wholly owned subsidiary in Dubai to target NRI, HNI & family office clients.

Angel One

Angel One Cons Inc came at 1,434 Cr, growing 25% YoY.

PAT increased 102% YoY to 231 Cr, while EBDAT increased 85% to 360 Cr.

Total income declined 2% QoQ, EBDAT declined 24% &Total orders also declined 6% QoQ.

Normalised EBDAT declined around 5% QoQ, while normalised margin remained healthy at 44%.

The average client funding book increased 5% QoQ to 6,140 Cr, while the closing book increased 31% to 7,150 Cr.

Interest income increased 31% YoY to 467 Cr.

Interest income now contributes around 33% of gross REV compared with 21% in Q1 FY25.

This represents a major shift in Angel One’s business mix.

Broking continues to acquire customers, while client funding is increasingly monetising the platform.

The funding book is granular. 83% of exposure is below 1 lakh, while 85% of the book has a duration below 30 days.

Angel One maintained an overall retail equity turnover share of around 20%.

Its F&O market share remained at 22%, while cash market share at 17%.

Wealth management business is scaling rapidly.

Ionic Wealth AUM increased 33% QoQ to 13,440 Cr.

UHNI families increased from 170 to 263, while total clients crossed 2,400.

AMC business is also expanding.

AUM increased 71% QoQ to 620 Cr across 11 schemes.

The business is still small, but Angel One is creating a complete product ecosystem across broking, funding, mutual funds, wealth, credit and asset management.

Angel One has 3.86 Cr users, with around 90% of new users coming from Tier 2, Tier 3 and smaller cities.

More than 45% of newly acquired users are below 25 years of age. This creates a long runway for cross selling financial products over the customer lifecycle.

Groww

Groww delivered the strongest, Total Inc increased 63% YoY to 1,549 Cr.

EBITDA increased 101% to 971 Cr, while PAT grew 94% to 735 Cr.

The key highlight was operating leverage.

Platform REV increased from 904 Cr to 1,473 Cr.

As a result, platform EBITDA margin increased from 55% to 68%.

Groww added 115k net NSE active clients in Q1, while the overall industry saw a net decline of 257k active clients.

Equity derivatives contributed 52% of REV compared with 56% last year.

Commodity derivatives increased from almost negligible levels to 5% of REV.

MTF contribution increased from 5% to 8%.

Commodity derivatives have emerged as a major new growth engine.

Active commodity users increased to 4.35 lakh from only 27,000 in Q2FY26.

Groww is the largest direct mutual fund distribution platform in India, with direct mutual fund AUM of 1.9 lakh Cr.

Its market share in mutual fund SIP inflows increased from 12% to 15%.

Groww AMC AUM increased 140% YoY to 5,491 Cr.

It has also received regulatory approvals for State Street Global Advisors’ strategic investment in the AMC business.

Overall, Groww delivered exceptional growth & significant market share gains across segments.

Key industry trend

Broking is becoming the customer acquisition layer.

Mutual funds, wealth management & AMC are becoming the retention layer.

Credit & insurance are becoming the cross selling layer.

Technology & AI are reducing the incremental cost of serving each customer.

Non-broking REV is increasing across all major platforms.

For Anand Rathi, MTF, distribution & other operating income now contribute almost half of REV.

For Angel One, interest income contribution has increased to 33%.

For Groww, MTF, commodities, mutual funds, AMC & credit are becoming increasingly meaningful.

MTF remains one of the fastest growing products across the industry

Anand Rathi Share & Stock Brokers’ MTF book grew 55%.

Angel One’s closing client funding book increased 31% QoQ.

Groww’s MTF book grew 264% YoY

The bigger opportunity lies in retaining customers and monetising them across MTF, mutual funds, wealth, AMC, credit and other financial products over several years.

items.title: The conversation is shifting. It used to be about how many ANDAs a company files which was a quantity game. Now it's about how many of those product l...
items.description_html:
The conversation is shifting. It used to be about how many ANDAs a company files which was a quantity game. Now it's about how many of those product launches actually make it out on time.

Indian biopharma has also stopped talking only about defending its US generics business. The bigger question now is how to fund pockets of incremental innovation back home in India.

That includes 505(b)(2) NDAs, sBLAs and drug repurposing, orphan therapies, combination products, proteins and peptides, and select specialty assets.

The prize is large with a global opportunity pool of roughly $400 bn, growing 9-11% a year. And the playbook is one that Indian companies already know well: widen prescription coverage, stack more patents onto each drug, use geography as leverage across EMEU, Asia, LatAm and Australia, and follow the patient across the full disease journey:- diagnosis, treatment, monitoring.

This is where the domestic market comes back into the picture. India's biopharma market at home is headed toward ~$42bn by FY30. Within that, chronic therapies are becoming a bigger share of the pie from ~39% in FY25 to ~47% by FY30, and ~52% by FY35. Growth is concentrating in a handful of therapy areas: cardiac, anti-diabetic, neuro/CNS, dermatology and respiratory.

And this concentration isn't random. New launches explain about 35% of it. Schedule-M and quality systems explain another 26%. Portfolio concentration adds ~22%, and sales-force depth adds ~17%. Put simply: the market is rewarding companies that can do all four things at once :- launch, supply, comply and commercialize.

That's the post-COVID recalibration in Indian pharma. It's not that India has suddenly matched the US or China on innovation depth. It's that the investment conversation has moved on from "who's the cheapest generic exporter" to "which companies can compound value through quality, filings, incremental innovation and therapeutic adjacency."

Source - Bernstein Research

items.title: People often find themselves in debt traps but this is not that bad. There's a 75 lakh home loan on a property worth 1.15 cr, so there's 40 lakh of eq...
items.description_html:
People often find themselves in debt traps but this is not that bad. There's a 75 lakh home loan on a property worth 1.15 cr, so there's 40 lakh of equity in it.

There's 75 lakh of savings, which is enough to service the other Other loans are 47 for her sister, and 22 of her own loans. If she sells just her house, she can wipe off all the debt and still be left with 50L plus 8L of gold or more.

That is not too much of distress for a single person aged less than 40

Expensive lesson perhaps on a US education loan, taking on more debt than cash flow allows etc. But not severe distress. Have to just sell your house and build for the future to buy another one later.


Abhishek Murarka 💹🐂: While listening to a Let's talk money podcast by @monikahalan, a user's complicated debt problem query came up. I think this is not to be brushed aside as just bad credit behavior - this raises concerns on the quality of underwriting, systems and checks.

items.title: Reuters: "The surge in bad consumer debt is mainly the result of ​last year's relaxed credit issuance to meet government consumption targets, said ba...
items.description_html:
Reuters: "The surge in bad consumer debt is mainly the result of last year's relaxed credit issuance to meet government consumption targets, said bankers with knowledge of the matter."

The only sustainable way to raise the role of consumption in driving economic growth is to raise the share of GDP that households are directly or indirectly able to retain. Temporary measures, like issuing consumption vouchers, forcing banks to expand consumer lending, or encouraging shopping holidays, only shift consumption, often from the future to the present.

If we are certain that future growth will take off, and drive a consumption surge with it, these temporary measures can be a good way to stabilize growth. But if growth instead slows, today's various "consumption boosting" strategies will only exacerbate the slowdown.
https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/chinas-record-consumer-defaults-undermine-beijings-push-boost-spending-2026-07-16/

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Every few months, a handful of Indian stocks quietly double or re-rate. The interesting part is that it has nothing to do wi...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Every few months, a handful of Indian stocks quietly double or re-rate.
The interesting part is that it has nothing to do with them being a "great business" or a "great story."

These extraordinary returns come from special situations that most investors either don't notice—or don't understand.
Demergers. Spin-offs. Delistings. Buybacks. Mergers. Holding company discounts. Open offers.

Some of the market's biggest wealth creators have emerged from these events.
The question is: How do you identify them before everyone else does?

This Sunday, we're hosting a special webinar with an expert on Special Situations Investing to decode one of the most fascinating corners of the stock market.

✰ Topic: Special Situations: Filtering Epic Ideas Through Corporate Actions with Ishmohit and Dhruv Bajaj
(Research Analyst — Equity bottoms-up, Event-driven ideas & Restructurings)
✰ Date & Time: 19th July, 2 p.m. (IST) onwards

items.title: Back in 2016–2017, Hero MotoCorp made its first major strategic bet on the EV maker Ather by investing roughly Rs 180-200 Cr for a ~28% stake (Valuat...
items.description_html:
Back in 2016–2017, Hero MotoCorp made its first major strategic bet on the EV maker Ather by investing roughly Rs 180-200 Cr for a ~28% stake (Valuation of Rs 635–700 Cr). Its cumulative investment is around 1700 Cr

Today, Ather is valued at 50700 Cr, and the same stake value is around 15210 Cr

They have made a 9x on their investment

Not just that, they are pumping in 1000 Crs more when they have their own brand, Vida

Ather is doing much better than Vida, and despite a 9x, Hero is investing, which speaks a lot about their conviction on Ather

no reco strictly

items.title: Venture asset class needs more “economic sophistication” and financial engineering in order win in the coming era as founders look to build bigger c...
items.description_html:
Venture asset class needs more “economic sophistication” and financial engineering in order win in the coming era as founders look to build bigger companies, according to Hemant Taneja of General Catalyst - which has backed Anthropic and Stripe.

“The last generation of venture won on qualitative advice and warm introductions; this one will win on capital.”

“The companies being built are different, too: infrastructure and hardware, not SaaS. To get there, they need to buy companies PE-style, build real infrastructure, and fund sales and marketing far past the antiquated Rule of 40 and the feeble CAC-to-LTV logic our industry clung to.”

“The best companies of the 2010s were forged in a free-money environment where moats were high and product was king. The winners of the next decade will embrace the limitless opportunities of the no-moats era, where the right capital structure, innovated well, becomes the moat itself.”


Hemant Taneja: http://x.com/i/article/2077398423997710336

items.description_html:
While listening to a Let's talk money podcast by @monikahalan, a user's complicated debt problem query came up. I think this is not to be brushed aside as just bad credit behavior - this raises concerns on the quality of underwriting, systems and checks.

The broad media headline is that Indian Banking NPAs are at historic lows, but has the credit cycle gone spurious in some pockets?

How was this Gold loan sanctioned where the end use was to service her Home loan emis and investments? While Gold prices softening, investors should be cautious on this pocket I believe.

items.title: RT Giannoni S.A.: China Is Sabotaging the World That Enables Its Rise #China #USA #Europe @michaelxpettis Breve resumen del articulo Las visitas de Es...
items.description_html:
RT Giannoni S.A.
China Is Sabotaging the World That Enables Its Rise #China #USA #Europe @michaelxpettis

Breve resumen del articulo

Las visitas de Estado consecutivas de Trump y Putin a Beijing en mayo de 2026 fueron leídas en Occidente como diplomacia rutinaria, pero la narrativa china las presentó como "misiones de tributo" al nuevo centro del mundo — Xi como el emperador que recibe a los potentados extranjeros, reforzando la idea de que China ha superado a sus rivales y recuperado su centralidad histórica

La tesis central del artículo es que China no ascendió a pesar del orden occidental sino a través de él: primero apoyándose en la URSS para construir sus bases industriales, luego explotando los mercados occidentales desde las reformas de Deng en los 70s para convertirse en superpotencia económica y militar. La paradoja es que Beijing parece ignorar que su éxito continuo depende del orden liderado por EE.UU. que está activamente erosionando

Xi adoptó el mantra "el Este asciende, el Oeste declina" (东升西降) como doctrina oficial, cuyas raíces intelectuales se remontan a la crisis financiera de 2007-08. Desde fines de 2025, memes virales chinos sobre el "American kill line" —la idea de que el estadounidense promedio vive a una crisis del colapso— refuerzan internamente esa narrativa, ofreciendo a una juventud china frustrada por la desaceleración económica la reconfortante certeza de que los problemas de China son temporales y los de Occidente son estructurales

Académicos como Jin Canrong de la Universidad Renmin sostienen que el centro de gravedad mundial se desplaza irreversiblemente hacia Asia. Este argumento ganó tracción en el mundo no occidental —Cambodia, Zimbabue, Irán, Arabia Saudita— y también entre líderes occidentales como Orbán y Fico, y en círculos empresariales y políticos influyentes de Francia, Italia y Alemania, donde el compromiso económico con Beijing sigue siendo preferido a la confrontación

En el cálculo estratégico de Xi, la "Trampa de Tucídides" puede evitarse porque EE.UU. se debilitará internamente antes de que cualquier conflicto catastrófico se vuelva inevitable. Beijing cree que donde la URSS fracasó por incapacidad de sostener la lucha económica e ideológica, China —con el "Socialismo con Características Chinas"— tiene la profundidad civilizacional, resiliencia política y dinamismo económico necesarios para superar la hegemonía estadounidense

El punto ciego fundamental de la narrativa china: a diferencia de la URSS, China nunca construyó un orden alternativo comprehensivo capaz de reemplazar las instituciones del sistema liderado por EE.UU. En cambio, dependió de la apertura del orden existente mientras desafiaba sus reglas desde adentro. Cuanto más debilita ese orden liberal, más socava los cimientos de su propia prosperidad

El modelo económico chino genera exceso de ahorro doméstico, consumo interno débil y superávits crónicos que obligan al resto del mundo a absorber su producción excedente. El economista Michael Pettis, con base en Beijing, argumenta que el ahorro doméstico suprimido y el exceso de producción forzaron a China a exportar tanto bienes como capital, requiriendo que otras economías —históricamente sobre todo EE.UU.— absorban los superávits chinos vía déficits comerciales y flujos de capital

Al desviar ese exceso de producción hacia los mercados europeos —práctica acelerada por los esfuerzos de EE.UU. de reducir su dependencia de importaciones chinas— Beijing expone a la UE a presiones de desindustrialización y competencia deflacionaria. Sin corrección, estos dinámicos generarán mayor desempleo, desigualdades sociales crecientes e inseguridad económica, alimentando el apoyo a movimientos nacionalistas y de ultraderecha en toda Europa

La respuesta proteccionista ya está en marcha a escala global: en Washington surgió un consenso bipartidario para reducir dependencia de cadenas de suministro chinas; la Comisión Europea declaró el estado actual de las relaciones económicas UE-China como "insostenible" y estudia medidas coordinadas sistémicas; India expandió instrumentos de defensa comercial y mecanismos de screening de inversiones; Brasil y Turquía intensificaron investigaciones antidumping; Indonesia busca reducir dependencia de manufacturas chinas importadas

Europa es el eslabón más vulnerable: la estructura del euro combina economías superavitarias exportadoras como Alemania y Holanda con economías más importadoras del sur, creando una divergencia de intereses frente a Beijing que China puede explotar para convertir presión económica externa en divisiones políticas internas. La amenaza de represalias sobre sectores altamente expuestos —la industria automotriz alemana, bienes de lujo, maquinaria, manufactura avanzada— debilita la capacidad europea de adoptar una estrategia coherente hacia China

Beijing descarta las preocupaciones de EE.UU. y Europa como intentos de culpar a China por sus propias dificultades económicas, argumentando que su superávit comercial refleja ventajas comparativas genuinas y elecciones voluntarias de consumidores y empresas occidentales. Pero esta postura representa una apuesta estratégica implícita: que el resto del mundo seguirá indefinidamente absorbiendo una dependencia cada vez más profunda de la capacidad industrial china — una expectativa que los autores consideran poco realista

La paradoja central del artículo: los mecanismos que sostuvieron el extraordinario ascenso chino están simultáneamente generando la resistencia internacional y la fragmentación sistémica que restringirán su desarrollo futuro. China quedó atrapada en una contradicción autorreferencial: cuanto más busca dominancia industrial global, más impulsa el proteccionismo y el de-risking que fragmentan el sistema económico internacional que habilitó su ascenso

La única salida estructural sería una redistribución masiva de ingreso hacia los hogares chinos para impulsar el consumo doméstico, reduciendo la dependencia de exportaciones e inversión industrial. Los autores la llaman, paradójicamente, "volver a ser más socialista —pero esta vez socializando la prosperidad en vez de la producción". El obstáculo es político: requeriría reducir los recursos controlados por empresas y gobiernos locales, algo costoso económica y políticamente para Beijing

El escenario más grave: si EE.UU. y Europa cierran decisivamente sus mercados al exceso de producción chino, China enfrentaría una restricción fundamental — los mercados alternativos en países avanzados son insuficientes y los países en desarrollo carecen de capacidad financiera para sostener los déficits requeridos. Bajo esas condiciones, China se vería tarde o temprano forzada a reconfigurar su modelo económico doméstico

La advertencia final de los autores: las grandes potencias enfrentan peligro cuando dejan de ver el éxito como producto de la contingencia y el esfuerzo, y comienzan a verlo como expresión del destino histórico. Si Beijing cae en esa trampa y rechaza las advertencias occidentales, desencadenará una guerra comercial devastadora que dejará al mundo en mucho peor situación y hará trizas el sueño de la "rejuvenación nacional" china

https://www.foreignaffairs.com/china/china-sabotaging-world-enables-its-rise a través de @ForeignAffairs

items.title: RT menaka doshi: Khorakiwala’s Innovation Playbook Wockhardt has just produced India’s first domestically-discovered antibiotic to win approval from...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
Khorakiwala’s Innovation Playbook

Wockhardt has just produced India’s first domestically-discovered antibiotic to win approval from the US Food and Drug Administration.

For over 15 years, founder and chairman Habil Khorakiwala poured roughly $300 million into a scientific gamble that many would have abandoned.

The result is Zaynich, a novel intravenous antibiotic designed to treat complex urinary tract infections. Wockhardt estimates the drug could eventually generate annual sales of as much as $2 billion.

Seated in a sprawling office overlooking the Bandra Kurla Complex business district, Khorakiwala described an uncommon approach to the risk of backing such a drug. Rather than viewing it as a single large gamble, he focuses on eliminating individual causes of failures.

“It’s like cricket,” he said. “You don’t think about scoring a century. You play one ball at a time. That’s how I manage what others perceive as enormous risk. To me, it becomes manageable.”

His account of Zaynich’s journey shows India’s biggest constraint in innovation is not a shortage of entrepreneurial ambition. It is the absence of a public-private ecosystem in which success depends on more than, as Khorakiwala put it with a smile, “some mad fellow like me.”

Read the full interview here (gift link)
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-07-02/indian-innovation-shows-proof-of-life-with-wockhardt-s-antibiotic-breakthrough?accessToken=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzb3VyY2UiOiJTdWJzY3JpYmVyR2lmdGVkQXJ0aWNsZSIsImlhdCI6MTc4NDExODQ5MCwiZXhwIjoxNzg0NzIzMjkwLCJhcnRpY2xlSWQiOiJUSEpDMzJLR0lGUTgwMCIsImJjb25uZWN0SWQiOiI3QUI4MjYzRkQwQzU0OTBFODk3Qjc5QUJGNTREMDAxRiJ9.vkdTPoC9H2g9zJZc2pDk_JPlg1OpDcg3JQhPQWHCeS4

items.title: RT Richa Pinto: Juhu beach swamped by 1,800 tonnes of waste in 10 days after heavy rains: Juhu Beach has witnessed an unprecedented surge in solid was...
items.description_html:
RT Richa Pinto
Juhu beach swamped by 1,800 tonnes of waste in 10 days after heavy rains:

Juhu Beach has witnessed an unprecedented surge in solid waste over the past few days, with civic officials attributing it to the intense spell of rainfall that lashed the city over a short period earlier this month. While the beach typically receives 90-100 metric tonnes of waste on a non-monsoon day and around 120 metric tonnes during the monsoon, the daily waste load has now risen to nearly 180 metric tonnes, with the peak touching an unprecedented 350 metric tonnes in a single day. Over the past 10 days alone, nearly 1,800 metric tonnes of solid waste have been cleared from the beach by the BMC.

items.title: I read about Solar Radiation Modification (SRM) recently, and it's quite interesting Obviously, it is one of the most controversial ideas in climate s...
items.description_html:
I read about Solar Radiation Modification (SRM) recently, and it's quite interesting

Obviously, it is one of the most controversial ideas in climate science.

The concept is simple:

Reduce a small fraction of sunlight reaching Earth to cool the planet temporarily. Think of it as wearing sunglasses for the Earth.

Methods being studied include:

• Releasing reflective particles high in the atmosphere
• Brightening marine clouds
• Increasing the reflectivity of surfaces

It's important because global temperatures continue to rise, while emissions cuts alone may not act fast enough in the near term.

But here's the catch: SRM doesn't solve the root problem—it doesn't remove CO₂. It also carries risks such as changing rainfall patterns, affecting agriculture, and creating geopolitical challenges.

It isn't a replacement for decarbonisation. It is an emergency tool that scientists are debating whether the world should ever use.

The next decade could decide whether SRM remains a research topic or becomes a climate policy discussion

items.title: Since Results Season #Q1FY27 is on, be ready for a lot of stock specific action Volatility usually increases during this phase because positioning sta...
items.description_html:
Since Results Season #Q1FY27 is on, be ready for a lot of stock specific action

Volatility usually increases during this phase because positioning starts building around earnings expectations

Some stocks will move in anticipation of results
Some will move after results are announced

So you will see many stocks moving sharply up or down on a daily basis

There will be cases where results are good, earnings beat expectations, and still the stock corrects

There will also be cases where results look weak on the surface, but the stock moves up because outlook is better than expected

A stock can fall after good numbers if expectations were too high

A stock can rise after average numbers if the worst was already priced in

And similarly, don’t be surprised when a stock moves sharply or behaves unexpectedly

This is simply how stocks behave during earnings season. Stock level volatility will be insane.

Focus more on the business performance, commentary & forward outlook rather than one day price movement.

So please don’t ask “why is this stock down?” 😭 during results season

items.title: "You need to remain abreast of Beijing's policy dialogue to read the tea leaves of the Communist Party's intentions." I am surprised you are suggestin...
items.description_html:
"You need to remain abreast of Beijing's policy dialogue to read the tea leaves of the Communist Party's intentions."

I am surprised you are suggesting that the problem is that I don't know that Beijing believes it is serious about wanting to raise the role of consumption in driving growth. Of course I know. Everyone knows, and has known for years.

That's also what the Japanese wanted in 1986. But simply saying they want it isn't enough, and I would have thought that after several years of failure in China, it might have become obvious by now that there is a difference between what is politically desirable and what is economically likely.

Beijing wants to raise the role of consumption in driving growth, but it also wants to do so without undermining growth. That's the real problem, and eventually many more policy advisors will understand that low consumption is not just an accident, or something an absent-minded policymaker forgot to do. It is fundamental to the structure of the economy.

This means that to raise the consumption share of the economy, wishful thinking is not enough. As we saw with Japan in the 1980s and after the adjustment began, China cannot sustainably raise the consumption share of GDP by more than an insignificant amount without undermining its manufacturing competitiveness, and with manufacturing representing such a high share of GDP (even higher than property development at its peak), China also cannot raise the consumption share of GDP without slowing GDP growth.

I am still a little shocked that there seems to be so little interest in understanding why other countries have wanted to do the same thing and found it so difficult.


China Banking News: You need to remain abreast of Beijing's policy dialogue to read the tea leaves of the Communist Party's intentions.

Sure - economists like Li Daokui want local governments to continue to engage in fiscal spending to support growth.

This is especially imperative during a period

items.title: RT Pratik Bhakta: Merchant payment processor Stripe and global private equity major Advent is set to join hands to acquire PayPal, according to a Reut...
items.description_html:
RT Pratik Bhakta
Merchant payment processor Stripe and global private equity major Advent is set to join hands to acquire PayPal, according to a Reuters report which has quoted sources privy to the development.

PayPal, one of the pioneers of digital payments globally, has been facing a slowdown in growth since the rapid adoption of mobile payments driven by Apple Pay and others especially in the US.

In India, PayPal couldnt grab a share of the massive digital payments pie despite being active for so many years. It never managed to build a business on UPI as well. Regulations and lack of focus on the India market were two of the main reasons for this.

It exit the domestic payments biz to focus on cross-border in 2021. But even there PayPal is facing severe competition from domestic payment startups, who are playing an aggressive growth game.

For years PayPal dominated the global paynents sector with a superb checkout experience, stringent fraud checks and through a trust network of merchants.

But tech is no more a moat in this world, commissions are getting squeezed as well. Tough for companies like PayPal to drive through under such conditions.

Will watch out whether the deal will go through. The Reuters report says PayPal is yet to respond to the offer and it is very early.

But the signal to the market is very clear. Payments world over will see another wave of consolidation and this time it will be in the mobile payments space. India will see this wave as well over the coming months/years.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Biocon - Prominent domestic MFs buyer in yesterday's block - Seller was Mylan Interesting bull vs bear argument on the stock Bernstein...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Biocon - Prominent domestic MFs buyer in yesterday's block - Seller was Mylan

Interesting bull vs bear argument on the stock

Bernstein on Biocon
U-P, TP Rs 326
Believe block deal provided a safe exit for Viatris, but hardly changes Biocon’s earnings trajectory
Believe that street optimism seems to price in a resolution to financial stress & smoother succession; see neither as fully fixed for 3 reasons
(1) Innovation spend on Biologics (BBL) and Contract Research (Syngene) has yet to convert into topline enough to support the billion dollar plus leverage on b/s
(2) Flag that policy shifts are lowering competitive barriers, structurally diluting moat that protected Biosimilar franchise for years, thus Biocon will require a minimum four
to five years to cross the $2 billion revenue threshold
(3) Succession still appears anchored around science-led bets rather than sharper commercial & capital-allocation leadership.

DAM Cap in Biocon
Buy, TP Rs 495
Mylan has exited its entire 5.64% stake in Biocon through a block deal at Rs400/share, removing a long-standing overhang on stock
Apart from Mylan, 3 other investors were also issued shares of Biocon as part of this restructuring process
Do anticipate some of these investors to monetize their stake though the timing of the remains uncertain at this point.
While H2FY26 had been mixed, believe Biocon’s biosimilar business has turned corner & is now well placed to build on this momentum
Expect business to strengthen meaningfully from H2FY27, driven by the scale-up of bUstekinumab, bAspart and bDenosumab, along with the launch of bAflibercept
Expect 15% revenue CAGR over FY26–28 with 26–27% EBITDA margins.

@CNBCTV18Live @CNBCTV18News @ekta_batra

items.title: Respectfully disagree. IBM and Indian IT pack are not the same. Let this past data tell you: IBM (1999-2008): Nil returns Infosys (1999-2008): 16x ret...
items.description_html:
Respectfully disagree. IBM and Indian IT pack are not the same.

Let this past data tell you:
IBM (1999-2008): Nil returns
Infosys (1999-2008): 16x returns

In the 2000s, the trio of TCS, Infosys, Wipro were the disruptors to the incumbents IBM and HP. The business strategies are also not same anymore.

Possibly, this trio is incumbent now, while the more agile midcap IT names chip away market share. AI has a lot yes to prove.

In short, I don't expect similar panic in Indian IT pack tomorrow, and a nice juicy dip would be a buying opportunity.



Rajesh Sawhney 🇮🇳: What happens to IBM today. happens to TCS and Infosys tomorrow.

items.title: Recode Studios Acquires 51% Stake in Premium Beauty Brand Aflairza Recode Studios is strengthening its Beauty & Personal Care (BPC) portfolio with the...
items.description_html:
Recode Studios Acquires 51% Stake in Premium Beauty Brand Aflairza

Recode Studios is strengthening its Beauty & Personal Care (BPC) portfolio with the acquisition of a controlling stake in premium beauty brand Aflairza.

- The brand has demonstrated superior value realization, reflecting its premium positioning

- Acquisition aligns with Recode's strategy to expand its presence in the high-growth Beauty & Personal Care category

- Recode plans to leverage its product development capabilities to accelerate Aflairza's growth

- Company will also utilize its distribution network and operational expertise to scale the brand

- Management aims to strengthen Aflairza's market presence across India

- Founders will continue to partner with Recode in the next phase of expansion

- Management's long-term vision is to build one of India's leading premium beauty businesses

It gives Recode a stronger presence in the premium beauty segment, where higher pricing power and better margins can create long-term value. Leveraging Recode's distribution, product development and execution capabilities could significantly accelerate Aflairza's scale over the coming years.

Recode will initially acquire a 33% stake in Phase I for an aggregate consideration of ₹8.32 Crore, with equal allocation between promoter share purchase and fresh capital into the company.


Beat The Street: Recode Studios: From Shark Tank to Dalal Street

https://insightsbybeatthestreet.substack.com/p/recode-studios-from-shark-tank-to

Why?

~ At valuations of 14 PE on 9MFY26 earnings
~ Revenue, EBITDA, PAT grew by 46%/106%, 118% on CAGR basis during FY23-25
~ Margins expanded from 12% to 23%
~ Works on an asset-light model with a focus

From gigafactories to global partnerships: 🔋

Tata’s Agratas is tapping advanced battery expertise to scale it's 60 GWh global ambition⚡

Agratas, Tata Group’s UK battery unit, has paid over £40 million (₹480 crore) to AESC Apollo across FY25 and FY26, according to UK filings. These milestone-linked payments are part of a deeper technology partnership to accelerate Tata’s EV battery and energy storage plans.

Key Details:
The payments secure critical battery technology know-how and intellectual property.
Runs alongside AESC’s separate investment, infusing ₹475 crore into Agratas Energy Storage Solutions (AESS) to maintain its 12% stake.
Milestone structure with significant payments already made in FY25 (£31 mn) and FY26 (£10 mn).

Agratas is building 20 GWh in Sanand (Gujarat) and 40 GWh in Somerset (UK), facilities designed to produce advanced battery cells for both electric vehicles and grid-scale Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS).

This positions Tata strongly in the fast-growing stationary storage segment, which is critical for India’s renewable integration and 24x7 clean power goals.

Tata Power is also actively deploying BESS projects alongside this cell manufacturing push, creating a strong end-to-end play across the battery value chain.

Meanwhile, India’s EV adoption continues its upward curve, with states like Odisha, Delhi, and Tamil Nadu leading in two-wheeler and passenger vehicle registrations.
Tata is building serious depth, from cell-level know-how and gigafactories to real-world energy storage deployment.

An interesting long-term move in a capital-intensive, high-potential sector.

What are your thoughts on Tata’s battery + storage strategy?

@TataCompanies @TataMotors

By: @kartik_ayaan

#Tata #Agratas #EV #BatteryTech #BESS #EnergyStorage #IndiaEV #MadeInIndia

items.title: RT ANI: #WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra | CareEdge Ratings Chief Economist Rajani Sinha says, “If you look at the latest data, the trade deficit has wid...
items.description_html:
RT ANI
#WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra | CareEdge Ratings Chief Economist Rajani Sinha says, “If you look at the latest data, the trade deficit has widened in June, and it's because of all components. It's not just oil imports which flared up in June, but it's also because of a sharp increase in non-oil imports and also because of a rise in gold imports… I would say that while trade deficit we expect it to remain high, overall we expect CAD (Current Account Deficit) to still remain comfortable… Overall exports have grown by a good 16%. The important point to note is that the very good growth in non-oil imports is a good indication… We are expecting CAD in the range of 0.8 to 1.2% of GDP, which basically means that we are factoring in good growth in goods exports. We expect a healthy momentum in services exports to continue, and also remittances have remained healthy. So broadly, I would say that even with trade widening and trade deficit widening, I don't find it very concerning. CAD is likely to remain in the range of 0.8 to 1.2% of GDP in FY27.”

items.description_html:
Across most of my investment interactions, a question almost always comes up - which sector looks good to you?

I try to talk my investment framework (which is sector agnostic - more horizontal than vertical), and the person abruptly asks - "Banks look good, right?" (Almost wanting me to say yes).

I find no right or wrong answer to this question. Well, they don't look bad to me - credit growth is improving, FCNR likely to help (atleast sentimentally).

But the space doesnt excite me naturally to say thumpingly - "Yes, buy Banks." Valuations have sobered over the years, but for real reasons. I admire this series of posts by Amit below and what exactly has been pressuring the valuation for Banks, even though profits grew. Any serious investor should read them.

items.description_html:
Growth is returning in a big way for domestic pharma

Pharma: Domestic sales – Sharp growth pickup in Q1; volume driven

The Apr-June IPM data points to a broad-based acceleration in the domestic formulations market, driven by continued outperformance in chronic therapies, improving volume contribution and healthy growth across companies.

◦ Strongest monthly growth in last several quarters at 16% YoY for IPM

◦ The strong June performance also lifted IPM Q1FY27 growth to ~14% YoY (vs the high-single to low-double-digit trend seen over the past several quarters), marking a clear inflection in the domestic formulations market.

◦ Chronic therapies continue to lead outperformance
· Growth remained chronic led, with chronic therapies growing at 20% YoY vs 13% for acute

◦ Healthy double-digit growth across companies in June/Q1FY27

Q1FY27 - with Sun (+15%), Cipla (+15%), Mankind (+13%), Torrent (+16%), Lupin (+17%), Dr. Reddy's (+15%), Alkem (+14%), Glenmark (+18%) and IPCA (+12%) reporting healthy growth. Among MNCs, Abbott (+14%), GSK (+11%), Pfizer (+12%), Sanofi (+10%), PGH (+15%) and AstraZeneca (+47%) continued to post strong growth

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: Last week, I was fortunate to visit the workshop (the Tamil word pattarai பட்டறை is so appropriate) of the leading motor desig...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
Last week, I was fortunate to visit the workshop (the Tamil word pattarai பட்டறை is so appropriate) of the leading motor designer in the world Morihiro Saito-san.

Saito-san is 80+ and still designing advanced motors, some of the most advanced in the world. His modest looking workshop is right next door to a company that builds out those motors in prototype quantities and he works closely with them to get them built. I visited as an industrial "pilgrim", with as much devotion as I go to the Thoranamalai Murugan Temple at home. When I saw him at work, I was moved to touch his feet.

Saito-san, the God of motors, is how Japan is able to retain its global prominence in so many foundational industries.

We need to learn from that spirit. We need to revive our "pattarai" culture of building things - every village in India needs such workshops!

My industrial pilgrimage to Japan has given me new vigor 🙏

items.title: Here's how copper recycling value chain looks like - moves from sourcing the raw material through processing and finally finished product delivery 1. ...
items.description_html:
Here's how copper recycling value chain looks like - moves from sourcing the raw material through processing and finally finished product delivery

1. Scrap sourcing (input)
The process begins with globally sourced copper-bearing wire and cable scrap rather than clean domestic copper cathode. Driven by the global replacement of older telecom infrastructure with fibre-optics, this cheaper scrap is harder to process due to plastic and aluminum contaminants. Effectively pricing and selecting these quality-varying lots requires expertise built over decades of supplier relationships.

2. Segregation and pre-processing
Incoming scrap is sorted and stripped of materials like plastic, aluminium, and tin prior to melting. This stage generates significant byproduct volumes (such as plastic granules), which recyclers utilize as secondary value-add opportunities.

3. Melting and refining
Sorted scrap is melted and fire-refined across furnaces to achieve near-zero oxygen content, a critical requirement for specialized electrical and mechanical applications. High-end producers rely on continuous-casting technology originating from Finnish suppliers to manage this quality differentiation.

4. Casting rod and ingot
Refined copper is cast into low-margin, commodity-tier rods or ingots sold to traders, contractors, or cable manufacturers. Versatile producers operate this as a swing business, running it during favorable spreads or redirecting capacity to higher-margin products.

5. Drawing and rolling (semi-finished forms)
Cast rod is processed through dies into fine wire or rolled into strips. Requiring low-oxygen copper feed, this step differentiates the metal into feedstock for specialized applications like motor windings and transformer components.

6. Coating and fabrication (value-added stage)
This high-margin stage involves coating wire or strip (enamel, tin, or silver) and fabricating them into components like busbars and Continuously Transposed Conductors (CTC). Plating choices balance cost and reliability: tin offers cost-efficient corrosion resistance, while premium silver plating is deployed for high-conductivity, zero-downtime needs like AI data-centre infrastructure.

7. Testing and quality assurance
Critical products like CTC undergo intensive inline testing regimes because applications in data centres and power transformers have zero tolerance for field failure. A single defect can compromise the entire conductor.

8. Distribution to end markets
Finished products are supplied to OEMs in electrical, automotive, renewable energy, and data-centre sectors. Economics compress downstream: commodity rod/ingot margins sit at 1–3%, while multi-process, value-added products command 5–10%+, with data-centre busbars yielding nearly double the blended average.

Spanning from sourcing to fabrication, a recycler managing this full process captures value at every step compared to those stopping at basic commodities. This structural logic explains the focus on a high value-added product (VAP) mix.

India ranks 176th among 177 countries in 2026 Environment Performance Index, a global assessment of environmental health, ecosystem vitality and climate change mitigation.

items.title: An elevator pitch on how the industrial gases industry works! You separate air into gases, or you buy rare gases and repackage them. You sell either f...
items.description_html:
An elevator pitch on how the industrial gases industry works!

You separate air into gases, or you buy rare gases and repackage them. You sell either from a plant you own (merchant) or from a plant you build inside a customer's factory (on-site).

On-site is a 15-20 year annuity at thin margins. A merchant is a spot market where the same molecule earns very different money depending on the gas, the distance, and who else is selling nearby.

The whole game is capital, location, and the ability to squeeze extra high-margin gas (argon, liquid) out of a plant you've already built.

But let’s get into interesting details about that -

What does the industry actually sell?

Strip away the chemistry and this is an air-separation business. A cryogenic plant is an Air Separation Unit, or ASU that chills atmospheric air until its components liquefy at their different boiling points, and pipes off oxygen, nitrogen and argon at high purity. Everyone in the industry runs broadly the same technology, so purity differences are marginal (roughly plus-or-minus 5%). The molecule is a commodity. Almost everything interesting happens around the molecule in how it's contracted, delivered, and priced.

The demand base has historically been the heavy stuff: steel and metals, autos, pharma, healthcare, and a thin slice of chemicals and F&B. What's changing the growth story is the sunrise demand from solar and semiconductors, which pull large volumes of nitrogen and, in semis, demand purity that cheaper suppliers struggle to hit. That single shift is why every player in the country is pouring capital into new capacity at the same time.

The top four global MNCs Linde, INOX Air Products, Air Water India and Air Liquide India control roughly 80% of the market.

Below them sit regional, owner-driven players (Ellenbarrie, Vayu, Shree Ram Oxygas and similar) who have been around 10–15 years and whose space widened after COVID, when oxygen subsidies pulled local capital in.

Now let’s understand the 2 engines in this industry separately > on-site vs merchant

Everything downstream flows from one fork in the road: do you build the plant inside the customer's factory, or near a cluster of customers and truck the gas out? These are two genuinely different businesses with different economics, different customers, and different personalities.

> On-site - An anchor customer, usually a large steel plant, issues an RFQ saying, in effect, we need ~1,100–1,800 tonnes of gas a day flowing into our furnace. A supplier builds and operates an ASU on their premises under a Build-Own-Operate (BOO) contract running 15–20 years. The revenue is fixed and stable, so it's underwritten on a modest return — players will accept an operating margin of roughly 10–12%, sometimes pricing the whole thing to a ~10–15% IRR. It is deliberately boring money.

Why fight so hard for boring money? Because it's stable, long-dated and defensive with a 15–20 year foothold that also keeps a rival out of that customer. Contracts signed before roughly 2016–18 carried fatter margins and some still run (a few end around 2035), but the new-build market is now openly competitive on price.

> Merchant business - Here you own the plant, liquefy the output, and sell it as liquid, bulk or cylinders to whoever's within reach. Margins are structurally better but volatile: liquid/bulk runs a ~20–25% bottom line, while cylinder and specialty can reach ~40%. The catch is geography. Oxygen and nitrogen are cheap and heavy, so you can't economically truck them much beyond ~200–300 km before distribution cost (typically 10–12% of revenue) eats you alive. You plant an ASU, draw a 200–300 km radius, and fight for that circle.

The reason branded MNCs earn ~40–45% at the top while a strong regional player earns ~35% isn't a secret formula rather it's scale and mix. A Linde or INOX moves 4,000–5,000 TPD daily; a regional player moves 250–300 TPD. At a small scale, tender-driven medical demand keeps margins decent. But to scale from 300 TPD to 3,000–4,000 TPD you must chase the lower-margin sectors that make up the bulk of the market and that's exactly where pricing and margin pressure begin.

The value chain, end to end

Follow a molecule of oxygen from the atmosphere to the customer and you can see where value is captured and where it leaks.

Stage 1 - Build the plant (the capital gate)
This is a capital-intensive, power-intensive industry, and the entry cost is the first moat. A modest 200–250 TPD on-site plant needs at least INR 300–400 crore of initial capex. Core components are compressors, cold boxes that have to be imported; India makes none of them. Only fabrication and storage tanks (INOX, Cryolor, VRV) are domestic. Build time runs 16–24 months per ASU

Stage 2 - Choose your engineering partner (the quiet moat)
Who actually builds the plant is becoming the real differentiator. The MNCs carry in-house EPC muscle - Linde has Linde Engineering, INOX leans on Air Products' US technology which lets them build large, reliable ASUs and win the technically demanding jobs.
Regional players without that engineering wing increasingly tie up with Chinese OEMs on a sale-of-equipment basis: the Chinese firm builds and exits, and the local player does the O&M. It's cheaper (a 200 TPD plant runs ~INR 100–120 crore via a Chinese vendor vs ~INR 150–200 crore via an MNC), but blue-chip steelmakers (Tata, JSW, SAIL, AM/NS) often won't allow a Chinese machine where they're underwriting 15–20 years of reliability, power efficiency and capital backup, and they pay up for it.

The cost-vs-capability gap
> MNC plant, 200 TPD: ~INR 150–200 crore — technology, reliability, blue-chip acceptance.
> Chinese plant, 200 TPD: ~INR 100–120 crore — cost-optimised, favoured by smaller / secondary steel and rolling mills.
> No Indian regulation restricts Chinese ASUs, so the split is driven purely by which customer is buying.

Stage 3 - Extract the by-products (where margin hides)
A basic ASU makes oxygen and nitrogen. To pull argon you must bolt on a separate argon-recovery column and here's the quirk: that column costs roughly the same whether the plant is small or large, but a small 200 TPD ASU yields only ~5–6 TPD of argon while a big ASU yields 50–60 TPD. The capital-to-recovery maths favours scale heavily, yet argon's economics are so attractive that even smaller players are now installing the column anyway.

Stage 4 - Distribute (the tyranny of distance)
Oxygen and nitrogen are regional products which are heavy, cheap, and uneconomic beyond ~300 km. Argon is a national product valuable enough to ship across the country, though distribution then runs ~17–18% of revenue rather than 10–12%. This distinction quietly shapes the entire competitive map: oxygen/nitrogen competition is local and radius-bound, while argon competition is national and cyclical.

How to judge any player in this industry?
Pulling the mechanics together, a handful of questions separate a structurally strong operator from a fragile one regardless of the name on the door.

> Capacity trajectory: Can they actually double liquid capacity? You can't sell a molecule if you can't make expansion of 200 TPD takes 1.5 - 2 years, not six months.

> Mix quality: How much on-site annuity vs volatile merchant? How much argon and by-product upside are they extracting per plant?

> Engineering independence: In-house EPC, or dependent on Chinese OEMs who build and exit? This gates which customers they can win.

> Geographic competition: Are their plants in pockets with few rivals inside the 300 km radius, or in contested western/central India?

> Blue-chip acceptance: Can they clear the reliability/purity bar for Tata, JSW, AM/NS, and semiconductors or are they confined to secondary, cost-driven demand?

> Cycle timing: Are they adding capacity into the current glut, and can their balance sheet and geography absorb 2–3 years of merchant price erosion before demand catches up?

Dislaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell
